questions.prompt: Which word is the MOST formal equivalent of the phrasal verb "find out"?

options.text: discover
get
ascertain
look up

questions.explanation: "Ascertain" is the most formal equivalent — a Latinate word used in legal and official writing. "Discover" is neutral but not the most formal choice. "Get" is informal; "look up" is another phrasal verb, equally informal.

options.text: Replacing nouns with pronouns to avoid repetition.
Converting verbs or adjectives into nouns to create a more formal, impersonal style.
Adding -ing to a verb to make it continuous.
Giving objects proper names in academic writing.

questions.explanation: Nominalisation is the conversion of verbs or adjectives into nouns (decide → decision; significant → significance) to achieve a more formal, impersonal, and abstract style typical of academic and bureaucratic writing. The other options describe unrelated grammar processes.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written in the MOST formal register?

options.text: We looked into the problem and found out what went wrong.
The investigation led to the identification of the root cause.
They kind of checked the issue and sort of figured it out.
We looked at the problem and saw what happened.

questions.explanation: Option (b) uses nominalisation (investigation, identification), Latinate vocabulary, and impersonal passive constructions — the hallmarks of formal register. (a) uses phrasal verbs (looked into, found out); (c) is informal with hedges (kind of, sort of); (d) is neutral but verb-heavy and personal.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a feature of INFORMAL register?

options.text: Passive voice constructions
Complex noun phrases
Hedging language (it has been suggested that…)
Contractions and phrasal verbs

questions.explanation: Contractions (it's, don't) and phrasal verbs (look into, give up) are markers of informal/colloquial register. Passive voice, hedging, and complex noun phrases are all features of formal/academic writing, not informal speech.

questions.prompt:
Rewrite to increase formality: "We need to look into why sales have gone down."
Choose the BEST formal version.

options.text: An investigation into the decline in sales is required.
We should check why sales got worse.
We kind of need to find out about the sales going down.
It's important to look at the drop in sales.

questions.explanation: Option (a) uses nominalisation (investigation, decline) and an impersonal passive construction (is required) — fully formal. (b) replaces one phrasal verb with another informal one (got worse); (c) is informal with hedges; (d) still uses a phrasal verb (look at) and contraction (it's), which lower the register.
